dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for chest pain at 1752 Apt on West Adams Boulevard.
Hi, I'm Marcus, an EMT with the Metro Ambulance. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain, started yesterday but got worse today around 1 before work.
Can you tell me your name and where we are?
Your eyes are open and you looked directly at me.
You understand me and answered appropriately.
Can you raise both hands for me?
Your airway is patent and clear, no obstruction present.
Are you short of breath or having trouble breathing?
I don't feel out of breath, but I do feel the chest pain more when I inhale and exhale deeply.
Let me check your pulse and blood pressure.
You're stable, no immediate danger at this time.
Can you tell me anything else about what's wrong?.
Do you have any allergies to medications or foods?.
What medications are you taking?.
Have you ever had any heart problems or surgeries?.
When did you last eat or drink?.
Can you describe what you were doing when the pain became worse?.
When was the first time you felt this pain?.
Does any movement make the pain worse?.
Can you describe the pain? It's difficult to translate, so just your best try.
Is the pain moving to other areas like your back or abdomen?.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how bad is the pain now?.
Has your pain been constant or has it changed over time?.
Pulse is 61 and normal.
Respiration rate is 18 breaths per minute with regular depth.
Blood pressure is 150 over 82.
Glucose reading is 112.
Oxygen saturation is 96%.
EKG shows sinus rhythm.
Skin is warm, dry, and intact with no discoloration.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
No jugular venous distension noted and no tracheal deviation observed.
No signs of drainage or blockage in the nasal or oral passages.
Breathing sounds are clear and equal in both lungs.
Chest rise is equal on both sides, and you're breathing normally with no signs of labored effort or use of accessory muscles.
No tenderness, rigidity, or masses found.
No pulsating masses detected and bowel sounds are present but otherwise unremarkable of concern or discomfort on palpation.
No injuries noted, and extremity movement is normal.
No deformities, abrasions, contusions, burns, or lacerations found during full external assessment.
Pulse, motor and sensation in all four extremities are present and equal.
We will be starting an IV for monitoring and support during our travel.
We see you have taken 628mg of aspirin, so we will not give you more now.
IV access has been successfully placed in the left arm via the antecubital fossa after one attempt.

12-Lead EKG is revealing some nonspecific ST and T wave changes in leads V1 and V5, possible moderate evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy, interpretable as sinus tachycardia with occasional premature ventricular contractions.
I am confirming the final EMS protocol for suspected cardiac chest pain on scene.
Double-checking IV access and notes on medications to confirm accuracy.
Pulse is now 84, still acceptable for a healthy 23-year-old.
Blood pressure is 140 over 86, still within normal limits for age.
You are alert and oriented to time, place, person, and situation.
EKG monitoring shows sinus tachycardia with some PVCs, but now we're measuring a return to sinus rhythm.
No signs of tracheal deviation seen at this time.
No puncture or lancing required in field due to intact skin and no signs of wound presence.
Speech is clear and no facial droop observed from multiple assessments.
You walked from the couch to the stretcher by yourself, a short 5-6 step distance.
We will transport you to VCU Medical Center, the designated site with appropriate ER resources ready.
Reaffirming no deformities, abrasions, contusions, burns, or lacerations found on physical assessment.
Pulse, motor and sensation in all four extremities are present and equal.
EKG monitoring shows sinus rhythm at this time.
You are now secured and ready for transport for further inpatient evaluation.
You are able to walk short distances independently on scene, indicating sufficient ambulatory ability at this point.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15; fully alert and oriented with no signs of altered mental status.
No signs of major bleeding or open wounds found during inspection of all extremities.
Bowel sounds are present, no abdominal tenderness or masses.
Equal chest movement is observed with normal breathing, no seen signs of distress.
Yellow ER Bed 19 at VCU Medical Center has been alerted and is ready for your arrival.
Sufficient English communication was achieved; no interpretation needed for medical processes or care report.
No headache or vision changes reported from prior assessment questions.
Pulse, motor, and sensation are present and equal in all extremities, per secondary and follow-up assessments.
EKG suggests possible left ventricular hypertrophy based on visual interpretation review.
ST-T abnormalities found in leads V1 and V5 during 12-lead EKG, as documented in medical report.
You were capable of short-distance ambulation on scene at the time of arrival and during initial transport considerations.
Your condition is stable, nothing seems to be worsening now per most recent assessment.
You may feel the monitor if we have it on, as it will track your heart rhythm, but everything is fine right now.
If you feel uncomfortable, let me know, but everything is fine right now, and your condition is currently stable.
We will make sure to document aspirin withheld, as patient already took 628mg.
We have all necessary medical details for transport and transfer of care.
We will assist you onto the stretcher now for ease of transfer into the ambulance.
You are now secure and ready for transport to the facility.
The hospital has been alerted, and the receiving facility has confirmed availability of Yellow ER Bed 19 for you.
I'll check in frequently during the ride to make sure you're okay, just to confirm stability.
Everything is ready for transport. We're starting our move now to the hospital.
Feel free to ask questions or share concerns at any time while en route for your comfort.
All findings and patient interviews have been clearly documented and ready for transfer.
Receiving facility signature has been obtained on the care report for full transparency.
